w:r.w:t: welcome everyone my name is nat trask and i'm here to talk about structure preserving scientific machine learning and how that can help microelectronics
co-design so when we talk about traditional design we have this hierarchy on the left in mind where
you're designing a system but you as a subject matter expert live on one rung of the ladder you design your component perhaps
optimally and then you take your design and you toss it over the fence to the person that's at the next rung up and that way information kind of
propagates in this one-way manner maybe with a few iterations until you've designed the whole system so microelectronics we're considering
systems which can consist of devices components and materials spanning quantum to continuum maybe to
digi digital discrete scales right and we want to get this system uh squeezed down onto a tiny footprint
and so when you go to small length scales the physics coupling each one of these the the linkages of this graph they
become tightly coupled and so you can't just do things in isolation you have to take a holistic viewpoint and design
everything all at once right and so this is what we mean when we say co-design as we'd like this sort of unified
means of simulating the the coupled behavior and so we have this well-established
software simulation stack right to simulate a material we do molecular dynamics or dft to simulate a component we think of tcap
models drift diffusion equations or maxwell's equations at the device level we think of compact models right kirchhoff's laws which are
encoded in odes systems odes to solve and so on up until you get your system scale model and so each one of these things is very
expensive right so a single one of these uh rungs of the ladder um would be a massive super computer simulation
but we can imagine that if somehow we could make it so that they were each free we could attempt to do a monolithic computation right where you couple them
all together and now you can see when you turn and knob on parameters of the material part how does that how's that reflected up through the entire
hierarchy and so what we're going to explore is how machine learning can be used to achieve surrogates uh that can achieve that goal
so where does one-way design break down um the material level you're talking about launching huge super computing campaigns
to do md or dft for a given material the component level say you want t-cad for a transistor you're talking about maybe up
to a million elements to simulate that single component and the system might have millions of transistors in it right
so that doesn't scale up so in order to scale it up you go to compact models for the device level these these uh like
kirchhoff's laws style circuit models often take decades to develop right so people are kind of empirically
up to a decade to develop the people are empirically trying to like add a diode here a capacitor there trying to get the right asymptotic behavior all
these kind of things which just takes time in order to develop right and so while this stack might work well um for traditional devices cmos and
things like that so you go to novel materials right like maybe your compact model no longer holds and you have to go back to the drawing board and start over
and now you've lost your entire simulation capability so at sandia we're looking at things like gann diodes 3-5
materials silicon photonics things like that where there isn't this kind of
established hierarchy or pipeline um and so also um in terms of fabrication we might design
a system but only be able to spin up one fabrication per year right and so there isn't a lot of trial and error here we don't get feedback in terms of
oh you were missing this interaction the skill the bridges scales in this way right we just get one one fabrication and maybe it fails
right so we need better tools and so we'd like to um you know like i said we want to bridge
this hierarchy we have this graph and so the linkages of this graph correspond to different things different kinds of inner device interactions
different multiphysics different devices components materials and so we'd like to use machine learning to
to surrogate out each one of these linkages and in order to do that these linkages need certain mathematical robustness guarantees
we need accuracy meaning that that machine learned model can be as arbitrarily accurate as our given application needs which isn't often the
case for uh deep learning you need stable coupling so if you learn one of these linkages in isolation it's guaranteed to be stable and predictive
and to generalize well once you hook it in and couple it together with all these other linkages and it needs to be structure preserving so probably most
importantly these models have certain physical invariances associated with them things like conservation structure and so on
and if this is meant to be a digital twin to help us achieve a design right uh it needs to predict a design that's physically realizable in practice right
we can actually fabricate it so there's a distinction i'd like to draw between traditional black box machine learning and scientific machine learning and there's all sorts of hype and excitement
a lot of it well deserved some of it not well deserved about people taking like their direct numerical simulation throwing into tensorflow all of a sudden
it's a thousand times faster but there's a difference between this black box picture right where really what you're doing is you have a set of
inputs and outputs you're training a network in order to reproduce this database and in the process building kind of a glorified
solution lookup table right there's no causal information there there's no physics modeling or subject matter expertise which would let
it be able to extrapolate right or perform out of out of distribution inference whereas what we're attempting with scientific machine learning
modeling is we're actually learning a physical dynamical system uh that matches the data and so what that means like if you want to
extrapolate right you want to look outside of the training set that you use to generate the model that's okay because in a sense you're applying different initial or boundary conditions
right the governing equations themselves are unchanged and so what that means is that if you're exploring this design space there's all these things that
could go wrong right and this provides guard rails right this can only suggest designs which are physically realizable
um and it keeps you from from wasting time where you know this kind of interpolant has suggested something that
can't actually be realized in practice so in my group we primarily focus on mathematical foundations of scientific
machine learning and the work i'm going to present today comes from my ido early career
project and also a mimic center then i'm a part of the george county dockets at brown but for the last year so we've been embedded in different microelectronics
co-design problems around the lab where the electrical engineers and the physicists are telling us this is what we need to design these are the physics
that are at play and this is how we need the machine learning to work and then we went off and we developed tools that attempted to meet those needs
so in the process of doing this we've considered a wide range of physics in different applications i'm not going to talk about all these today but things
like data driven semiconductor models shock magneto hydrodynamics or the pulse power fusion devices at sandia uh subsurface
models saw mechanics and so on um so for all of these there's this common thread which is that if you'd like to perform high
consequence engineering like the kinds of things we do at sandia you need to develop trust in the sense that these data-driven
models need to have they need to be predictive like you need to know that um they're not going to go
wrong just because you've trained them in not quite the right way or if you know all those sorts of details right and so the idea is we'd like to take the
mathematical structure that you use to traditionally design forward modeling and simulation capabilities and work that into machine learning
i'm going to break this part this talk up into three parts first an example our co-design problem to show what we mean by a digital twin and hopefully
establish some requirements for how we need the machine learning to work in order for it to map on to reality then we'll get into that specific
mathematical machinery and what we've been developing there and finally in part three we'll take a maybe a forward-looking perspective and ask
questions about once you have this digital twin for you the device that you're interested in you're trying to design what can you do now that previously
might not have been feasible so this is the device we're going to consider it's an optical communications device it's a laser that sits on a
satellite while the the overall system itself is large at about a meter cubed um the laser is quite small and
what's taking up all this space is a mechanical system whose job is to point and steer the laser right and so this is meant to go up into
space we'd like it to be lighter and have a smaller footprint for that reason and so the idea here is we'd like to design a microelectronics device which
completely removes the mechanical system and does this all with silicon photonics so the challenge there is yes we've made
it small but we pay the price that we have all these different components to this design that are all so close to each other that their physics are
tightly coupled um so uh now to get into a sense of like what is this thing how does it work
um you it starts at this laser diode you apply a voltage drop and then recombination within the diode
generates photons and then photons combine into optical wavelength electromagnetic waves right so then those waves are pushed
into a data modulator which shapes the light in order to encode data right this is the signal that we're going to
be broadcasting using the laser and now we send that signal through splitters in order to obtain multiple
copies of the same signal so as you split it you reduce the power by half each time and now you need to feed it
into optical op amps in order to boost the power back up and now at this point toward the left here we're left with
multiple identical copies of the same signal and so what we like to do is engineer modulate
the phase of each one of those signals so that we can achieve constructive or destructive interference and that way we can use that as a
mechanism in in order to to point and steer the laser coming out and in order to achieve that what we do is we apply a
voltage drop across these phase shifters that voltage drop introduces it induces a temperature differential
and that temperature differential modulates the uh the magnetic permittivity right and and that within
the silicon photonic waveguide and that's the way that we're able to to manipulate this thing is required but of course now this is a lot of moving parts
with a lot of different physics and they're all tightly coupled so let's go block by block to get a sense of how they're working this is the
laser the laser diode and so if you were to do forward simulation for this you would solve the semiconductor equations
like drift diffusion equations which have a recombination term here which prescribes how photons are
generated and those act as a source within maxwell's equations right and this is how these photons are converted within the laser into
electromagnetic waves that are pushed through the waveguide okay and so the requirement we have here for structure preservation is as as you
go through these different representations of light right you need to hand off from one kind of physics to the other kind of physics to the next one in a way that you're not
violating conservation of energy um so that's one notion of structure preservation another building block that we're going
to have to build is this phase shifter so in this phase shifter right this um like i said we're inducing a thermal
gradient so that means we need to simulate the thermal physics within this component and then those thermal physics uh
modulate the permittivity right so that's the material property within maxwell's equation and so here we need this tight coupling
between uh electromagnetism and in the thermal physics in order to get this control logic right this is the
mechanism that we're using in order to to shape and steer the beam itself right so we need a good coupled multi-physics representation
now let's zoom out and look at the device overall so there's a geometric question about where you're going to stick each one of these components on the device
and as you squeeze everything down to a smaller and smaller footprint on the bottom left here we see that as the device gets smaller
the temperature rises okay and now in this middle picture this is showing exactly what happens uh thermally and how that impacts the
system you have the term the terminal which is applying voltage just to drive things at this indium contact and so
that has a thermal bloom right uh associated with it and now that thermal bloom is affecting the permittivity and
that changes the optical response within the waveguide right so this thermal physics is actually degrading the performance of the device and the way we
address that we mitigate that in the design is by manipulating or engineering the material part of the system so we can
introduce an uh epoxy under fill with desirable heat transfer properties and in that way we can steer it back into the realm where things are working
properly so as you can see right this spans the all all of this hierarchy that we've been talking about and you need to get each one of these
components simultaneously in order to solve this design problem finally i'd like to call attention to uh this
the the the fact that um uh so shown here at the top of this figure is a side view the top half of this side
view you see all the silicon photonics but underneath it you have uh the digital logic and the power supply right like the piping and the infrastructure
that's running this actual system and so if you want to embed some sort of uh digital twin in here to do some sort of optimal control
or or whatever it is you want to use machine learning for you can't stick a gpu cluster in there to do inference on your neural network right it's big and
the whole point is to make it small so this calls in a question exciting opportunities for different kinds of edge computing right where you can take
novel low-power uh learning architectures things like neuromorphic computing and embed them alongside this so that you can do
inference of this digital twin for whatever your task might be okay um so now hopefully that gives a sense of what it is we're trying to
achieve and why it's so important to get these individual guarantees from block to block now we're going to get into the math and these three ingredients that i'm going
to introduce to to get this right and we have this sort of archetype to keep in mind so what we're trying to do is to build a
database of high fidelity simulations perhaps parameterized by some design variable that lives at the quantum scale or the atomistic
scale we're going to use machine learning to partition these into
to partition space into physics and form subdomains and now we can use a graph in order to encode those subdomains so nodes correspond to
the the volumes edges correspond to the interface between two volumes what we would like to learn is a model which encodes the
the fluxes or the circulations right how these subdomains interact with each other and what we're after here is something that doesn't just fit the data
well but give certain guarantees so that now when you take this graph model and you embed it in a circuit simulator like
spice right it's guaranteed to be stable and reliable and it's not going to make the rest of you know your simulation
pipeline explode the first ingredient we'll discuss is accuracy and so in traditional modeling and
simulation grid refinement is a bedrock of verification and validation you need to show that if you refine your solution
twice right the answer doesn't change so for neural networks you don't have that so as you go to a deeper network or a wider network
your solution eventually will stagnate and you're stuck in a local minimizer and so you have some some fraction of a percent of accuracy
typically for a range of different learning architectures and so we've been doing a lot of research to develop new architectures which have these kind of
convergence guarantees and accuracy guarantees and the idea here is we're going the same way that a finite element mesh
would partition space and let you do piecewise polynomial approximation we build we use classification networks in order to build a hierarchy of
partitions of space but this is totally mesh free right there's no human in the loop or mesh generation and then on each one of those things we can fit a
polynomial model we have fast optimizers in order to do it and you can train this hierarchy with a v cycle thing that goes up and down so if you want to
approximate this piecewise smooth function the dash blue line here what's happening is it's finding these partitions of space and it drives the
error down to machine precision so say you want to do something like build a data driven model for a gan diode
this is in forest forward bias mode and you have data either from simulation or experiment for iv curves across this device
so this is challenging because at low voltages you have this ideal diode like exponential scaling whereas at large voltages you
have this resistor like linear scaling and so this is a very challenging non-parametric regression problem spanning five orders of magnitude and so
this hierarchical viewpoint is able without a human in the loop right you just feed it a stack of iv curves it spits out a bunch of machine learning
models you can go off and embed next we'll turn to physics preservation
and so in forward simulation of electromagnetism edge and phase fine elements or ravier toma or netellic
finite elements are like the cornerstone of how to get predictive simulations and so what those what we're after here
is to design a graph neural network architecture which encodes the exact same kind of information right so we're going to engineer that architecture the
same way you would traditionally design a finite element space and so what these fine elements you know this will give the same sorts of guarantees that you get out of finite
elements things like relevant conservation structures preserved you get a formal stability analysis that lets you
make guarantees about how this is going to extrapolate and then you'll get a numerically stable model and most importantly for e m is that you
get this memetic treatment of the involution condition right you need the magnetic field to be exactly divergence free in order to be productive so what
we extract here is basically machine learnable div grad and curl like from the familiar vector calculus analogs to that
with the property that the divergence of the curl is equal to the curl of the gradient and that's equal to zero and i'm not going to get into the math
here i'll refer um to this other lecture at the bottom of the page if you're interested there what this gives you when you have that exact
sequence structure as it's called is you get a machine learnable duram complex which preserves all of this algebraic structure and it gives you certain
building blocks that you can use and so what is how do we build a model out of this right this lets us pose you know
you can make onsots that there is a conservation principle underlying the fields that you'd like to machine learn
and then uh you're going to machine learn fluxes here and so we're going to machine learn these generalized fluxes in terms of a
diffusive flux this is like working with artificial viscosity this gives you like a nice elliptic stable
building block that facilitates analysis and then you have this black box neural network thing that we assume no a priori structure for and so we we learn our
physics as a non-linear perturbation of a diffusion problem and that lets us prove lots of things about it um
including that a solution exists it's unique and stable and that lets us do things like work with equality constraint optimization and so that's
how we can exactly enforce physics in these kind of architectures and contrast the things like physics and foreign machine learning which is popular
and also useful in its own way but only enforces physics by penalty so here's an example where we've used
this to build a surrogate for lithium lithium-ion batteries so on the left here this is a ct scan we
have a voxelized representation of a microstructure blue is lithium particles orange is a conductive binder white is a structural
matrix and so we have tools at sandia to automatically build meshes from these voxelized microstructures and it's a
super expensive thing even for this simple 2d problem we're looking at about 6 million elements but that tells you exactly how
uh the electric field how the uh the lithium concentration how how that all percolates through this microstructure and gives you these pathways
and so what our framework gives us is a way of extracting a finite element space that that preserves these transport
pathways gets all of this conservation uh correct but and we can reproduce this high fidelity simulation using only
eight elements while maintaining under one percent error so that means we we can we've extracted a model where now you're just
solving an eight by matrix instead of a six million matrix and so that's essentially free right so now we can use this finite element and couple it to
other things to you know if you want to design like a battery pack for example and this is how we can scale up the microstructure finally we'd like to couple across
scales and so there's a lot of work going on at sandia um led by people like in thompson mitch
woods fedeslaf nikolov where you'd like to use like some physics you need a density functional theory type
description of the physics uh thermomagnetism is one of those kinds of things and so what they've been
working on and making a lot of progress on is how to machine learn a molecular dynamics potential designed to reproduce
those physics that live on the quantum scale and so here we're they're learning spin dynamics right so now you have a model for the magnetic spin that lives
on each atom which would traditionally be something at the dft level and so what we can do is you know you go from the quantum to the molecular you do a
gigantic in lamps a gigantic molecular dynamic simulation of what's called a domain wall so you have a thermal bar
and you flip the spin on one end and watch magnetic wave propagate through and now we can use that as data to train our data driven finite element model and
so in this way we have we've built up this stack so that you can go from the quantum to the molecular to the continuum model and you can bridge that
linkage going from the material up until the device so finally i'm gonna we're gonna turn to this question about
now if you have this digital twin what exactly can you do with it and so there's the obvious kind of thing that you can do
where you have this digital twin you can do forward simulation so for a given design you can simulate the behavior of that
design and you also look at inverse problems right so you have all these parameters on that design you can calibrate those in order to maximize or
minimize some performance metrics some quantity of interest all right so that's one thing you can do with this and and this is why we were so
careful to put all these knobs on these surrogates right each one of these design parameters um
uh now we can tune but we can see how it affects the performance at a system level of everything all at once so we
can optimize some sort of interaction with the environment in this case it would be some property of the beam how diffuse it is
things like that um you can also use this to do feedback control right so you can put a thermocouple on your device you could say this part of the device is running
hot dial down the voltage in this area and look for strategies like that um but maybe more abstractly to go
beyond that sort of traditional modeling and simulation um forward and inverse modeling kind of paradigm you can ask about how you could actually
do artificial intelligence right so not just probe a specific design but can we prompt new designs or can we ask for
information that can facilitate scientific discovery so there's a lot of different tools within machine learning that could be
brought to bear here one in particular that we've been looking on particularly for additive manufacturing and material systems
is this idea of multi-modality so in traditional computer science you can imagine you have this picture this abstraction of a bird here on the
left and that abstraction could be encoded in different modalities so you could have a picture of that bird you could have a sentence describing that bird and what the human brain can do is
it can integrate all of these disparate types of information totally heterogeneous and integrate them in a way so that they're greater than the sum of their
parts and so that's used to do things like uh fuse audio and text data to automatically build subtitles right and
so for this talk you could turn the subtitles on and that's a machine learning algorithm that's that's blending those two modalities so in in science and engineering we have
different kinds of multimodal data that hasn't really been tapped into yet you have high fidelity modalities that are difficult slow to collect in large
number um kind of low throughput things like microscopy x-ray things probing microstructure
you know physical experiments or costly synthetic experiments in terms of direct numerical simulation and so on there's also low fidelity modalities
that you can gather in large number in a high throughput way and these are things like all the diagnostics all of like
the the the current coming off of your fabrication things that are going on um just during the the typical manufacturing process we'd like to uh
integrate all of these to build a multi-modal digital twin and so just last december there was an ai a doe workshop that said this type of
multimodal scientific machine learning is one of the most promising directions within doe for the next decade
and so what we do here is we integrate all of these different modalities in this this autoencoder kind of
framework and encode them into a latent space so that they share a single common representation which we model through a
gaussian mixture model right so this means that each one of these modalities is being encoded into a single cluster where the mode the different modalities
share some common information right and this is probabilistic right so we have a uq framework where we can assign a probability to belong to each one of
these clusters and so now we decode that and we ask that it's able we we embed these physics and form
surrogates that we've been building that we introduced at the beginning of the talk and so what you're asking for is a
sieve a way of digging through all of these different modalities such that you get a representation
that encodes the underlying physics that are shared across these different modalities i'll refer to this paper in the bottom left for how we actually
achieve that so here is an experiment that we did this is a metal additive lattice we printed two different properties
you put it through a compression test and so the two modalities that we have here are images of the microstructure along with this physical the sort of
scientific data response in terms of the stress strain curves and so when you run this algorithm on the left you can see each black dot is corresponds to a pair
of these two modalities the red dots are clusters and you can see it's swapping these black dots back and forth until
it's figured out the two subpopulations in the data and then what it's doing is all the way on the right it's using this
physics model to fit a linear strain hardening model to the to the data right so you're asking for
these two subpopulations so that you get it in terms of the mechanics of the problem but now you can go back and do generative sampling so you can say what
exactly do those what microstructure corresponds to those two different physics at play and it says this is what the
microstructure looks like there are these two different things so you can use that as a way to prompt hypothesis generation for scientists right there might be too many modalities for a
human to kind of dig through this will pop up like here's a fingerprint this says what's at play
here so we'll end on this note um i'd like to close with a couple topics for the breakout discussion
um the first is um you know in your world uh what kind of microelectronics co-design problems or maybe not even
microelectronics right what co-design systems are you familiar with that you think this sort of digital twins perspective is feasible and where
you can see how you can break it down into the scientific machine learning blocks uh second
where where do you view the current capability gaps right there's a lot of hype here a lot of machine learning tools and not all of them are really
ready for showtime what we're interested in doing is fleshing out the idea of trusted scientific machine learning not just doing the latest and greatest kind
of machine learning but being principled and really bringing in the ingredients that we need so that we can in a reliable way
perform high consequence engineering design so i'll close there thanks a lot and i'm looking forward to
joining you in the breakout discussions thanks
